title: Depth Estimation Datasets and Evaluation
concept: Training and evaluating depth models requires ground-truth distance data, typically gathered from LiDAR, structured-light, or stereo rigs, and evaluated using metrics that account for depth's continuous, scale-sensitive nature.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why shouldn't a robotics team relying on millimeter-precision assembly tasks use only monocular depth estimation?

options.text: Monocular depth is a learned statistical estimate, generally less precise than true geometric methods needed at that precision
Monocular depth is always exactly as precise as any other method
Depth estimation is entirely unnecessary for assembly tasks

exercises.explain: Yes! High-precision tasks typically need more precise sensing than monocular alone.

cards.title: You need a ruler to grade a ruler
cards.body: To train a model to estimate distance, you need TRUE distance measurements to compare against during training and evaluation — you can't teach or grade depth prediction using only regular color photos with no ground truth.

cards.title: Ground-Truth Depth Sources
cards.body: Training and evaluating depth models requires ground-truth distance data from real sensors: LiDAR (laser distance measurement, common in self-driving datasets), structured light (projected patterns, used in early depth cameras), or precise stereo rigs calibrated to produce reliable disparity-based ground truth.

cards.title: A tape measure behind every photo
cards.body: Imagine every training photo in a depth dataset comes paired with an actual tape-measure reading of the true distance to every point in the scene. That's essentially what LiDAR or structured-light sensors provide — a trustworthy 'answer key' the model's predictions get compared against.

cards.title: KITTI and NYU Depth datasets
cards.body: KITTI (captured with LiDAR-equipped self-driving cars) and NYU Depth (captured with structured-light depth cameras indoors) are widely used benchmark datasets pairing regular photos with precise ground-truth depth maps, letting researchers train and fairly compare depth estimation models.

cards.title: Depth errors aren't judged like classification errors
cards.body: It's a myth that depth evaluation works like simple right/wrong classification accuracy. Because depth is continuous (a prediction can be 'close but not exact'), evaluation uses metrics like mean absolute error or relative error, which reward predictions that are numerically CLOSE to ground truth, not just exactly correct.

cards.title: Depth Ground-Truth Sources
cards.body: Comparing common ways to collect true depth data:

left.text:
Laser-based distance measurement
Very accurate, works outdoors
Common in self-driving datasets

right.text:
Projects a light pattern, reads distortion
Accurate indoors, short range
Common in early depth cameras

exercises.prompt: Why do depth estimation models need real sensor data like LiDAR for training and evaluation?

options.text: To provide ground-truth distance measurements the model's predictions can be trained and compared against
LiDAR is only used to compress image file sizes
Regular color photos always already contain distance labels
Ground-truth data is unnecessary for training depth models

exercises.explain: Correct! Ground-truth distance data is essential for supervising and evaluating depth predictions.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Depth estimation evaluation typically uses simple right/wrong classification accuracy, just like image classification.
exercises.explain: False. Since depth is continuous, evaluation uses metrics like mean absolute error that reward numerically close predictions, not just exact matches.

exercises.sentence: KITTI and NYU Depth are widely used benchmark ___ pairing photos with ground-truth depth maps.
exercises.explain: These are standard benchmark datasets for training and evaluating depth estimation models.

exercises.prompt: Why is mean absolute error a more appropriate depth evaluation metric than simple exact-match accuracy?

options.text: Depth is continuous, so a prediction that's numerically close to the true distance should be rewarded, not just exact matches
Mean absolute error cannot be computed for distance measurements
Exact-match accuracy is always the correct metric for any continuous value

exercises.explain: Correct! Continuous-valued predictions need continuous-valued error metrics.

exercises.prompt: A research team wants to train a new monocular depth model but only has access to regular color photos with no distance information at all. What is missing from their setup?

options.text: Ground-truth depth data (e.g. from LiDAR or structured light) needed to supervise training and evaluate the model's predictions
Nothing; depth models can be trained using color photos alone with no ground truth
A second camera is strictly required even for monocular methods
GPU hardware is the only missing requirement

exercises.explain: Exactly! Ground-truth depth data is essential — without it, there's nothing to train or evaluate against. Next: where depth estimation actually gets used in real products.
